pages.full_text:
We had lunch sitting at the roadside on the grass. Charles is now just old enough to do this safely.
	We went to the beach at Lland[?]g. The [Cttis?] Church is the earliest in Britain, c.650. Built for St. Patrick's mission at Wales. The church, with our church, were having a barbque.
	Men were riding noisy motorbikes in the waves. John & Charles bought buckets & spades & Peter bought a water-pistol. We sat on damp sand & had an icecream. Lovely shells.
	When we got back to the campsite, Imogen was there with Tom & Caroline.
	Charles tried sleeping in the tent, but woke up & cried. So he's sleeping in the van, but John is sleeping in the tent.
__Mon 25th May__
	We all climbed into bed for Peter to open his presents. After 45 minutes homework we all went off on a tricky walk – 2 miles of bog[?]
pages.notes: Page continues from previous. The beach place name reads 'Lland[?]g' with an abbreviation mark — likely 'Llandudno' or 'Llanddwyn' (Newborough, Anglesey); the church described as earliest in Britain c.650 built for St. Patrick's mission points toward Llanddwyn or a site in Anglesey/Wales. The annotation before 'Church' is unclear — could be 'Celtic' or 'Cttis' (abbreviation). Year cannot be confirmed from this page alone; no year written in margin. The 'Mon 25th May' date at bottom leads into text that continues on the right page.

pages.full_text:
After we returned the Hudfords arrived in time for Peter's birthday cake. The children play on the rope swing. 9 of us set off through the wood & across the fields for 8 pints of milk. 1½ hours later we returned with only one pint because the dairy is being painted. Later the Hudfords drove Thomas & Simion for some.
	We had a lovely leisurely barbeque. The Hudfords had just bought a boat.
__Tues 26th May__
	Very hot again as soon as we wake up. The boys set off to play on the rope swing. After more homework, Simon, Eve, Thomas, William & Peter set off on a walk, leaving Laura to look after John & Charles for the day.
	It was very, very hot. William in a foul mood – heaving rocks & thrashing bracken. Finally Simon allowed him to walk home on his own. We saw him from a great distance high up – bright yellow – striding
pages.notes: Page number 113 is partially visible at top right corner. Text continues from left page (Mon 25th May entry) then begins 'Tues 26th May' entry. Year not written on page; cannot confirm year from content alone. 'Hudfords' is a surname (family name), not a place. 'Simion' spelled as written — may be 'Simon' misspelled or a different person. Text cuts off mid-sentence at bottom ('striding') — continues on next spread.
